name: Richard Strauss
abstract: Richard Georg Strauss (; German: [ˈʁɪçaʁt ˈʃtʁaʊs] ; 11 June 1864 – 8 September 1949) was a German composer and conductor known for his tone poems and operas. A leading figure of the late Romantic and early Modern era, he combined, along with his friend Gustav Mahler, subtleties of orchestration with an advanced harmonic style.

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: He walks in and he says, “Mr. bass trombone player, if you must practice it, the melody goes [Baker sings the famous French horn melody from Richard Strauss's composition Till Eulenspiegel].”
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: Richard Strauss. Not Johann. Johann is okay. He did all of the waltzes. But Richard Strauss, that composer, boy. You ever hear the Hero’s Life – Ein Heldenleben?
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Hyman
about_quotes.evidence: I like to listen to classical music... it might be Richard Strauss in particular. Strauss was wonderful.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Hyman interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: I like to listen to opera, especially by Verdi and Puccini and Richard Strauss, Richard Wagner.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Clyde Kerr Jr.
about_quotes.evidence: It’s sort of like some of the music of Richard Strauss, he had like programmatic music.
about_quotes.doc_title: Clyde Kerr Jr. interviewed by Monk Rowe, Rome, New York, August 22, 2006

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Candoli
about_quotes.evidence: you know when he wanted Richard Strauss, pull out the file and — or like anybody.
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Candoli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999
